data.title: Copper prices slide on Trump’s reciprocal tariffs; Freeport-McMoRan sinks 7%
data.description:
U.S. copper futures slump nearly 4% to three-week lows on Thursday after President Trump unveiled his plan for an array of reciprocal tariffs, ratcheting up a trade war that threatens to hurt the global economy and demand for raw materials.

The tariffs are viewed as more aggressive than expected, with a 10% duty on all exports to the U.S. and even higher rates on ~60 nations, including 34% on China and 20% on the European Union; commodities including energy, steel and aluminum were exempted for now, although tariffs on copper are expected within weeks.

Front-month Comex copper (HG1:COM) for May delivery -3.9% to $4.844/bbl, and shares of top copper producers trade broadly lower, including Freeport-McMoRan (NYSE:FCX) -7.1%, Southern Copper (SCCO) -4.6%, Teck Resources (TECK) -6.7%, Hudbay Minerals (HBM) -5.6%, Ero Copper (ERO) -5.4%.

Diversified global miners show milder declines: BHP (BHP) -2.1%, Rio TInto (RIO) -1.1%, Vale (VALE) -0.5%.

ETFs: (CPER), (COPX), (OTC:JJCTF)

Domestic copper prices had increased YTD beyond those in the rest of the world as traders and manufacturers raced to bring metal into the U.S. ahead of tariffs, but today's decline suggests that traders now expect weaker demand for copper.

data.title: Acuity anticipates growth in intelligence spaces and tariff impact mitigation
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Acuity (AYI) Q2 2025

Management View

  • CEO Neil Ashe highlighted the company's steady performance in Q2 2025, with growth in net sales, adjusted operating profit, and adjusted diluted earnings per share. The company rebranded from Acuity Brands to Acuity to reflect its scalability and future focus.
  • ABL's electronics portfolio was emphasized, with investments in drivers, lighting controls, and software, aiming to enhance product vitality and operational efficiency. Recent product launches include the GOTHAM IVO Deep Regress Downlight and Cylinder, which integrate Acuity's lighting controls for optimized aesthetics and energy efficiency.
  • Acuity Intelligent Spaces (AIS) saw strong sales growth and began integrating QSC into its portfolio. CEO Ashe noted disruptive technologies in AIS, including Atrius, Distech, and QSC, as key drivers of smarter, greener spaces.
  • CFO Karen Holcom reported Q2 net sales of $1 billion, a year-over-year increase driven by AIS growth and QSC performance. Adjusted operating profit increased to $163 million, with a margin improvement to 16.2%. Adjusted diluted EPS was $3.73, up 10% from the prior year.
  • ABL's adjusted operating profit grew despite slight declines in sales, with strategic pricing initiatives mitigating market uncertainties.

Outlook

  • Management reaffirmed its guidance from Q1, including expectations of $4.3 billion to $4.5 billion in full-year 2025 net sales and adjusted diluted EPS ranging from $16.50 to $18. CEO Ashe emphasized confidence in the company's ability to manage evolving tariff impacts through strategic pricing and supply chain dexterity.
  • AIS's focus remains on growth and margin expansion, leveraging its unique combination of technologies to enhance end-user outcomes.

Financial Results

  • AIS recorded $172 million in Q2 sales, with a notable contribution from QSC. Adjusted operating profit for AIS reached $32 million, reflecting an 18.7% margin.
  • Cash flow from operations reached $192 million fiscal year-to-date, with effective capital allocation towards the QSC acquisition and shareholder returns, including a 13% dividend increase.
  • ABL's performance was impacted by retail and corporate account declines, offset by growth in independent and direct sales channels.

Q&A

  • Chris Snyder, Morgan Stanley: Asked about tariff impacts and competitive positioning. CEO Ashe described tariffs as a supply shock, emphasizing Acuity's global supply chain flexibility and strategic pricing actions to manage financial impacts.
  • Ryan Merkel, William Blair: Inquired about market uncertainties and competitive advantages. CEO Ashe and CFO Holcom highlighted strategic pricing and the integration of QSC as factors supporting growth.
  • Joe O'Dea, Wells Fargo: Sought clarification on guidance and integration progress. CFO Holcom confirmed no changes to core guidance, with QSC synergies expected in the future.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts expressed concerns over tariff impacts and market uncertainties but acknowledged the company's proactive measures and strategic initiatives.
  • Management maintained a confident tone, emphasizing the strength of its supply chain and strategic pricing flexibility to navigate challenges.
  • Compared to Q1 2025, sentiment reflected increased focus on mitigating external pressures while advancing growth strategies.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Q2 guidance remained consistent with Q1, reflecting stability despite external market uncertainties.
  • Strategic focus shifted towards integrating QSC and expanding AIS's disruptive technologies.
  • Analysts' tone in Q2 reflected heightened interest in tariff impacts and pricing strategies compared to Q1's emphasis on foundational performance improvements.

Risks and Concerns

  • Tariffs pose potential supply chain and cost challenges, though management is addressing these through strategic pricing and supply chain adjustments.
  • Market uncertainties, including project delays and economic conditions, were noted as influencing demand, particularly in ABL.
  • Analysts queried the potential for customer hesitation due to tariff negotiations.

Final Takeaway

Acuity demonstrated robust performance in Q2 2025, with strong results in AIS and strategic integration of QSC driving growth. Management's proactive approach to tariffs and strategic pricing initiatives supports confidence in navigating market uncertainties. Forward-looking efforts to expand disruptive technologies in AIS and maintain operational efficiency highlight Acuity's commitment to long-term growth and shareholder value.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Morgan Stanley’s takeaways on tariffs: as much as 22% for the effective tariff rate if stackable
data.description:
If tariffs announcements prove to be durable, there will be a meaningful weight on growth, according to Morgan Stanley.

The downside risks to the economy will be larger if these tariffs remain in place, said Michael Zezas, Morgan Stanley head of U.S. public policy research.

“Assuming tariffs are stackable, the effective tariff rate could be as high as 22%, compared to 3% at the start of the year,” he said, and added that risks to inflation are now to the upside. “We think tariff-induced inflation will keep the Fed on the sidelines, and we remove our June rate cut.”

Morgan Stanley analysts now forecast no rate cuts in 2025 and for the rate cutting cycle to begin in March 2026. The terminal rate, they said, will stay at 2.50-2.75%.

President Donald Trump’s Wednesday announcement on tariffs “only reinforces our opinion that restrictive trade and immigration policies, along with uncertainty related to fiscal policy, are likely to weigh meaningfully on the outlook for growth,” said Zezas.

“Risks to our outlook for growth skew more meaningfully to the downside if these tariffs remain in place for a non-trivial period of time, and risks to our outlook for inflation are weighted to the upside, particularly over the next 3-6 months.”

He also said that he doubts that deregulation and fiscal policy measures will offset the drag from trade and immigration policy and boost economic activity.

Here are his assumptions on effective tariff rates:

Assuming that the announced tariff rates are not stackable on top of existing ones, that the USMCA-compliant trade is exempt, and that tariffs on autos between Mexico and Canada are also exempt, the effective tariff rate would be about 16% – twice as much as Morgan Stanley previously assumed in their baseline.

Also, assuming tariff rates are not stackable, that USMCA-compliant trade is exempt, and that 25% tariffs on trade in autos between Mexico and Canada stay in place, the effective tariff rate would be 17.3%.

Assuming all announced tariffs are stackable – with some reports indicating that the 34% tariffs on China imports will be added on top of the prior 20% – the effective tariff rate could be as high as 22%.

“Today’s decision will, at a minimum, double the effective tariff rate in our central scenario,” Zezas said. “We previously did not expect tariff rates to peak until later this year or early next year.”

data.title: Nvidia downgraded at HSBC on 'limited' GPU pricing power
data.description:
• Nvidia (NASDAQ:NVDA) was downgraded to Hold from Buy at HSBC on Thursday, as the investment firm said it sees "limited" pricing power for GPUs going forward until several long-term bets start to emerge.
  • Shares fell 4.8% in early trading.
  • "We first upgraded Nvidia to Buy in April 2023 on the back of Nvidia's strong AI GPU pricing power and product mix being underestimated by the market that we believe has continued for past two years," analyst Frank Lee wrote in a note to clients. "However, we now think Nvidia's GPU pricing power is slowing down as we have seen no significant ASP boost between B200 and B300 GPU or the GB200 and GB300 NVL72 rack architecture. We also see limited spec migration outside of adoption of HBM4 memory with Nvidia's upcoming Vera Rubin which does not see any change in GPU headcount vs Blackwell, remaining at 72 GPUs per rack until Rubin Ultra in CY2027."
  • In addition to the downgrade, Lee lowered his price target to $120 from $175 as he cut his fiscal 2026 earnings and revenue estimates.
  • As for the aforementioned catalysts, Lee thinks the growth in robotics and autonomous artificial intelligence could expand Nvidia's total addressable market. But before that happens, there's likely to be a peaking in cloud service provider or possible slowdown from DeepSeek (DEEPSEEK), he posited.

data.title: Parker-Hannifin downgraded at Wolfe Research on macro concerns
data.description:
Parker-Hannifin (NYSE:PH) on Thursday was downgraded to Peer Perform from an investment rating of Outperform by analysts at Wolfe Research. They cited a combination of valuation concerns, macroeconomic uncertainty and potential downside to 2026 earnings estimates.

Shares of Parker (NYSE:PH) fell as much as 9.9% on Thursday morning, compared with a 3.6% decline for the Standard & Poor's 500 (SP500) stock index.

In a note to clients, Wolfe analyst Nigel Coe said that while Parker (PH) remains a high-quality company with a strong history of defending margins during downturns, the current risk/reward profile is balanced, particularly in light of the stock’s premium valuation and increasingly crowded investor positioning.

“We view the valuation as balanced at approximately 21 times next-twelve-month price-to-earnings, about $1 downside risk to fiscal 2026 midpoint guidance (in early August) and extreme crowding. Risk/reward is balanced,” Coe wrote.

Tariff-driven uncertainty clouds outlook

Wolfe’s updated 2025 outlook for the electrical equipment, multi-industry, distributor and industrial tech sectors flagged tariff-related macro uncertainty as a significant risk to the U.S. industrial recovery. Coe said industrial lead indicators no longer point to improving conditions, and a potential U.S. recession could place additional pressure on short-cycle demand.

“Parker’s industrial business is still cyclical, and we see increasing risks of a U.S. recession that could further impair industrial demand,” Coe said.

While the company has diversified significantly — aerospace now represents approximately one-third of the portfolio — Coe emphasized that Parker (PH) still has “short-cycle DNA,” which could make it more vulnerable to a downturn in industrial orders and revenues following recent strength in fiscal Q2.

Cautious EPS outlook for FY26

Coe flagged a possible $1 downside to fiscal 2026 EPS estimates, with Wolfe modeling a midpoint of $28.50, below the current Street consensus of $29.40. The firm’s model incorporates 3% to 5% organic growth, split between low-single digits in its industrial segment and high-single digits in aerospace, along with 35% incremental margins.

“Commercial Aero Aftermarket comps get tougher entering fiscal 2026, which leads to a trickier setup for PH overall organic growth,” Coe said.

Valuation not compelling

Despite Parker’s (PH) recent share price pullback, Wolfe sees the valuation as stretched relative to both the company’s own historical metrics and its sector peers. The stock currently trades at 21 times next-twelve-month price-to-earnings, slightly below its one-year median of 22 times, but above its five-year median of 18 times.

More critically, Parker (PH) now trades at a 5% premium to the EE/MI sector, compared with a 5-year median discount of 10%, suggesting a potential rerating risk if earnings expectations are revised downward.

Investor crowding adds to risk

Wolfe’s sentiment data also points to heavy investor crowding, with Parker (PH) ranking as the second most over-owned name in its latest quarterly survey. This concentration adds another layer of risk if sentiment begins to sour amid earnings pressure or a broader market selloff.

Coe’s revised year-end 2025 fair value range for Parker (PH) is $512–$735 a share, though Wolfe doesn’t assign specific price targets to Peer Perform-rated stocks.

“This could be a tricky setup for a name trading at a relative valuation premium to history with potential downward EPS revision risk,” Coe said.

data.title: BJ's Wholesale's tariff and trade-down advantage earns upgrade at Citi
data.description:
As one of the few industries that will benefit from a new tariff landscape and “trade-down” environment due to tighter budgets, BJ’s Wholesale Club (NYSE:BJ) was upgraded at Citi Research to Buy from Neutral with a corresponding bump in its price target to $130.

In a research report on the broadlines and hardlines sector, Citi warns of the "rising recession risk probability and a slowdown in consumer spending...[that is] a significant risk for our big ticket exposed retailers and achieving 2025 guidance."

Not so for BJ's Wholesale (NYSE:BJ), however, as Citi views the company as a "tariff & trade down winner." 

For BJ’s Wholesale Club (NYSE:BJ), the warehouse club business model positions the company positively as consumers search for value, while its low import exposure offers insulation from tariff risks.

Accordingly, while the broader market capitulates to the tariff turmoil and recession fears, BJ’s Wholesale Club (BJ) is trading higher for a fourth consecutive day with a new record high.

data.title: Biggest stock movers Thursday: TSLA, AAPL, BAC, NKE, and more
data.description:
Stock futures slipped on Thursday, with the Dow Jones dropping more than 1,000 points, as investors reacted to President Donald Trump's sweeping import tariffs on U.S. imports, raising concerns about a possible global trade war. The updated tariff structure sets a minimum rate of 10%, with the highest tariff reaching 49%.

Here are some of Thursday's biggest stock movers:

Biggest stock gainers

  • Lamb Weston (LW) +10% - Shares surged after the company posted strong FQ3 results, beating revenue and profit expectations, with adjusted EBITDA climbing 6%. The company reaffirmed its FY2025 outlook, projecting net sales of $6.35B to $6.45B (vs. $6.34B consensus), adjusted EBITDA of $1.17B to $1.21B, and adjusted net income of $440M to $460M, with adjusted diluted EPS of $3.05 to $3.20 (vs. $3.08 consensus). CEO Smith noted that Lamb Weston remains on track to reduce capital spending by $250M this year, with an additional $200M reduction expected in FY2026, bringing total reductions to $450M vs. FY2024.
  • Diageo (NYSE:DEO) +4% - Shares climbed as analysts viewed the U.S. tariff announcement as avoiding the worst-case scenario for the spirits industry. Goodbody analysts noted that no additional duties were imposed on imports from Canada and Mexico, making the outcome "less bad than feared" for Diageo. Jefferies analyst Edward Mundy highlighted that a potential 200% tariff on EU alcohol imports was averted, offering relief to European distillers.

Biggest stock losers

  • Tesla (NASDAQ:TSLA) -8% - Shares plunged after reporting its worst quarterly deliveries since 2022, falling significantly short of expectations. Tesla delivered just 336K vehicles in Q1, well below the consensus estimate of 377K, as production disruptions from the Model Y refresh and potential backlash against Elon Musk’s political involvement weighed on results. Wedbush analyst Dan Ives called the numbers a "disaster" and urged Musk to refocus on Tesla rather than political controversies and his ties to Dogecoin. Meanwhile, Politico reported that U.S. President Donald Trump has told insiders that Musk will step back from his role as a governing partner in the coming weeks.
  • Wayfair (NYSE:W) -14%, Nike (NYSE:NKE) -8%, American Eagle (NYSE:AEO) -5% - Shares of major retailers tumbled as the market reacted to the impending 46% tariffs on Vietnam announced by U.S. President Donald Trump. The steep tariffs, set to take effect on April 9, could significantly impact companies in the apparel, furniture, and toy industries, potentially driving up costs. Analysts warn that some corporations may pass these cost increases onto consumers through price hikes, adding to inflationary pressures in the retail sector.
  • RH (NYSE:RH) -26% - Shares plunged after the luxury home furnishing company missed Q4 estimates and provided weaker-than-expected guidance for FQ1 and FY2025. RH cited multiple headwinds, including tariff uncertainty, market volatility, inflation risks, and “the worst housing market in almost 50 years.” For Q1, the company expects revenue of $817.8M to $825.1M, below the $840.4M consensus, with an adjusted operating margin of 6.5% to 7.0%. FY2025 revenue is anticipated to grow in the range of 10% to 13% Y/Y, translating to $3.499B to $3.595B, missing the consensus of $3.64B.
  • Apple (AAPL) -7.6% - The biggest decliner among the Magnificent Seven stocks as President Donald Trump’s sweeping tariff measures rattled global markets. With a significant reliance on Chinese manufacturing, the iPhone maker faces increased exposure to the most aggressive U.S. trade restrictions in a century. The broader tech sector also suffered, with Amazon (AMZN) down 5.9%, Nvidia (NVDA) -5%, Meta (META) -4%, Alphabet (GOOGL) -3%, and Microsoft (MSFT) -2.3%.
  • Bank of America (BAC) -9.4% – Led a broad selloff in the banking sector as the KBW Bank Index (KBWB) plunged 7% at Thursday’s open, marking its worst decline since the 2023 regional banking crisis. President Donald Trump’s sweeping tariff measures rattled global markets, fueling concerns over weaker fee income and declining consumer sentiment. Other major banks also tumbled, with Citigroup (C) down 8.7%, Morgan Stanley (MS) -9%, Goldman Sachs (GS) -7.7%, Wells Fargo (WFC) -6.7%, and JPMorgan (JPM) -6%. The KBW Regional Banking Index slid 5%.

data.title: ConnectM to evaluate $46.5M buy-out offer
data.description:
• ConnectM Technology Solutions (NASDAQ:CNTM) has received a non-binding proposal offering $1.60/share in cash from its three largest institutional investors to acquire all remaining outstanding shares of the Co. and transition ConnectM into a privately held entity.
  • The ConnectM Board of Directors and management team are actively reviewing this proposal and have initiated a search for a leading investment banking firm to provide expert guidance in evaluating this buy-out offer.
  • ConnectM ensures a thorough assessment of this offer as regards its alignment with the Co.'s long-term objectives and the best interests of all Co. stockholders.
  • The proposed transaction, if accepted, would result in the privatization of ConnectM and is subject to the completion of customary due diligence, definitive agreements, and regulatory approvals.

data.title: Amneal gets expanded insurance coverage for its Parkinson’s disease treatment
data.description:
Amneal Pharmaceuticals (AMRX) announced Thursday that insurance coverage accounts, the Veterans Administration, UnitedHealthcare (NYSE:UNH) and CVS Health (NYSE:CVS), have added Crexont extended-release capsules for Parkinson’s disease treatment to their national prescription drug formularies.

The New Jersey based biopharma company said that this expansion increased Crexont’s total U.S. insurance coverage from about 30% of covered lives at the end of 2024 to over 50%.

As far as the latest additions are concerned, Veterans Administration has already added the product in its national formulary, UnitedHealthcare will add it to its standard prescription drug lists in the second quarter of 2025, while CVS Health will add Crexont as a “preferred” product to its national formulary on July 1, 2025.

CREXONT is a prescription medication for the treatment of Parkinson's disease, which received regulatory approval in the U.S. in August 2024.

data.title: ISM Services PMI weaken more than expected in March, employment index slides
data.description:
March ISM Services PMI: 50.8 vs. 53.0 consensus and 53.5 in February, according to data released by the Institute of Supply Management on Thursday.

New orders: 50.4 vs. 52.2 in February

Prices: 60.9 vs. 62.6 prior.

Employment: 46.2 vs. 53.9 prior.

Business activity/production: 55.9 vs. 54.4 prior

Supplier deliveries: 50.6 vs. 53.4 in February.
